segments.title: North African forces depended on Mediterranean sailings
segments.summary: The Duisburg convoy was one of the regular Mediterranean sailings that sustained German and Italian forces in North Africa.
segments.body: Clarke presents this convoy as part of a recurring Axis supply route rather than an isolated voyage. German and Italian forces in North Africa depended on the supplies carried across the Mediterranean, so disrupting a sailing had consequences beyond the cargoes lost in one action.

evidence.note: The transcript describes regular German and Italian sailings to resupply forces in North Africa.

segments.title: Codebreaking and Malta reconnaissance expose the convoy
segments.summary: Enigma and Italian codebreaking identified the sailing, while RAF reconnaissance helped locate it and distorted Axis estimates of Malta's strength.
segments.body: The convoy was identified through both Enigma and Italian codebreaking, then an RAF reconnaissance aircraft was sent to find it. Clarke notes that repeated successful sightings encouraged Axis forces to overestimate the reconnaissance resources and garrison available on Malta, showing how an intelligence advantage could also shape an enemy's assumptions.

evidence.note: The transcript links codebreaking, RAF reconnaissance, and Axis overestimation of Malta's reconnaissance capacity.

segments.title: Aurora, Penelope, and Upholder strike the convoy
segments.summary: Aurora and Penelope, with two destroyers and the Malta-based submarine Upholder, destroyed the convoy and sank about 40,000 tons of shipping.
segments.body: Clarke credits the light cruisers Aurora and Penelope and their two destroyers with wiping out the convoy in a single action. He estimates the loss at about 40,000 tons of shipping and adds that Upholder, a U-class submarine based at Malta, also joined the attack.

evidence.note: The transcript names Aurora, Penelope, their destroyers, the 40,000-ton loss, and Upholder's Malta base.
